domain:
A Person has a name and an identifier. Every Person is the child of another Person and can have an indeterminate number of children (however, there will be one Person who is not the child of any other Person). A Person has an indeterminate number of ancestors (the ancestors of a Person are their father and the ancestors of their father). We will be interested in knowing the degree of each ancestor of a Person (1 for the father, 2 for the father's father, etc.).

A Resource has a name. There are two types of Resources, such as Files and Folders. Every resource belongs to a Folder and cannot exist outside of a Folder (however, there will be one Folder that does not follow that rule, the root Folder does not belong to any other Folder). Each Folder contains an indeterminate number of Resources.

A User is a Person who, in addition to a name and an identifier, also has a password to access an indeterminate number of Resources, and each Resource can be accessed by an indeterminate number of Users. When a User can access a resource, we will be interested in knowing if they can view it and if they can edit it.

questions.question:
Should identifier be modeled as a separate class associated with Person, or remain as a simple representation?

For example:
1. Identifier is a separate class with its own attributes. This allows users to query and filter by individual identifier properties.
2. Identifier is modeled as a simple representation in Person. This is suitable when identifier details are not needed.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: The Identifier is a separate class with its own attributes.
questions.option_2: Option 2: The identifier is a simple representation in Person.
questions.selected: Option 2: The identifier is a simple representation in Person.

questions.question:
Should the identifier in person be modeled as a an attribute which allows free text input, or as an enumeration with predefined literals?

For example:
1. Use identifier as string attribute in person, which allows any text value.
2. Use identifier as an enumeration which uses predefined values in literals: ssn, employeeid, studentid or passportnumber.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: String attribute that allows any text value for identifier.
questions.option_2: Option 2: Enumeration identifier with predefined values: literal 1, literal 2 and literal 3.
questions.selected: Option 2: Enumeration identifier with predefined values: ssn, employeeid, studentid and passportnumber.

questions.question:
Should ancestor link be modeled as a separate class associated with Person, or as a simple representation within Person?

For example:
1. Ancestor link is a separate class with attributes: degree. This allows users to query and filter by individual ancestor link properties.
2. Ancestor link is modeled as a simple represntation in Person. This is suitable when ancestor link details are not needed.
Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: The AncestorLink is a separate class with its own attributes.
questions.option_2: Option 2: The ancestor link is a simple representation in Person.
questions.selected: Option 1: The AncestorLink is a separate class with its own attributes.

questions.question:
Should views be modeled as a subclass of access, or is it better to keep a simple boolean like access in view to distinguish them?
Consider whether views have or will have distinct attributes or behavior.

For example:
1. View is a subclass of access. This allows views to have their own specific attributes and methods.
2. View is an attribute in class access. All instances share the same structure, with the attribute indicating the type.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: Use inheritance, View as a subclass ofaccess.
questions.option_2: Option 2: Use view as a boolean attribute of  access.
questions.selected: Option 2: Use view as a boolean attribute of  access.

questions.question:
Should edits be modeled as a subclass of access, or is it better to keep a simple boolean like access in edit to distinguish them?
Consider whether edits have or will have distinct attributes or behavior.

For example:
1. Edit is a subclass of access. This allows edits to have their own specific attributes and methods.
2. Edit is an attribute in class access. All instances share the same structure, with the attribute indicating the type.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: Use inheritance, Edit as a subclass ofaccess.
questions.option_2: Option 2: Use edit as a boolean attribute of  access.
questions.selected: Option 1: Use inheritance, Edit as a subclass ofaccess.

questions.question:
What is the minimum cardinality of User in the relationship with Access?

For example:
1. Minimum cardinality of zero, an access can exist without any associated user.
2. Minimum cardinality of one, an access must have at least one associated user.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: Zero as minimum cardinality, Access can exist without User.
questions.option_2: Option 2: One as minimum cardinality,Access must have at least one User.
questions.selected: Option 2: One as minimum cardinality,Access must have at least one User.

questions.question:
What is the maximum cardinality of User in the relationship with Access?

For example:
1. Maximum cardinality of one, an access is associated with exactly one user.
2. Maximum cardinality of many, an access can be associated with multiple users.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: One as maximun cardinality, each access links to one user.
questions.option_2: Option 2: Many as maximun cardinality, each access can link to multiple users.
questions.selected: Option 1: One as maximun cardinality, each access links to one user.

questions.question:
What is the minimum cardinality of Access in the relationship with User?

For example:
1. Minimum cardinality of zero, a user can exist without any associated access.
2. Minimum cardinality of one, a user must have at least one associated access.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: Zero as minimum cardinality, User can exist without Access.
questions.option_2: Option 2: One as minimum cardinality,User must have at least one Access.
questions.selected: Option 1: Zero as minimum cardinality, User can exist without Access.

questions.question:
What is the maximum cardinality of Access in the relationship with User?

For example:
1. Maximum cardinality of one, a user is associated with exactly one access.
2. Maximum cardinality of many, a user can be associated with multiple accesses.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: One as maximun cardinality, each user links to one access.
questions.option_2: Option 2: Many as maximun cardinality, each user can link to multiple accesses.
questions.selected: Option 1: One as maximun cardinality, each user links to one access.

questions.question:
What is the minimum cardinality of Access in the relationship with Resource?

For example:
1. Minimum cardinality of zero, a resource can exist without any associated access.
2. Minimum cardinality of one, a resource must have at least one associated access.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: Zero as minimum cardinality, Resource can exist without Access.
questions.option_2: Option 2: One as minimum cardinality, Resource must have at least one Access.
questions.selected: Option 1: Zero as minimum cardinality, Resource can exist without Access.
questions.llm_raw_response:
Option 1: Zero as minimum cardinality, Resource can exist without Access.  

This option is better because the domain description indicates that a Resource can be accessed by an indeterminate number of Users, implying that it can exist without any associated access.

questions.question:
What is the maximum cardinality of Access in the relationship with Resource?

For example:
1. Maximum cardinality of one, a resource is associated with exactly one access.
2. Maximum cardinality of many, a resource can be associated with multiple accesses.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: One as maximun cardinality, each resource links to one access.
questions.option_2: Option 2: Many as maximun cardinality, each resource can link to multiple accesses.
questions.selected: Option 2: Many as maximun cardinality, each resource can link to multiple accesses.

questions.question:
What is the minimum cardinality of Resource in the relationship with Access?

For example:
1. Minimum cardinality of zero, an access can exist without any associated resource.
2. Minimum cardinality of one, an access must have at least one associated resource.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: Zero as minimum cardinality, Access can exist without Resource.
questions.option_2: Option 2: One as minimum cardinality, Access must have at least one Resource.
questions.selected: Option 2: One as minimum cardinality, Access must have at least one Resource.

questions.question:
What is the maximum cardinality of Resource in the relationship with Access?

For example:
1. Maximum cardinality of one, an access is associated with exactly one resource.
2. Maximum cardinality of many, an access can be associated with multiple resources.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: One as maximun cardinality, each access links to one resource.
questions.option_2: Option 2: Many as maximun cardinality, each access can link to multiple resources.
questions.selected: Option 2: Many as maximun cardinality, each access can link to multiple resources.

questions.question:
Should the relationship between Folder and File be modeled as a composition or as an association?

For example:
1. Use composition, File would depend on Folder for its existence.
2. Use association, File could exist independently of Folder, and can also be linked to multiple folders.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: Composition is needed because class File depends on Folder.
questions.option_2: Option 2: Association is sufficient because class File exists independently of Folder.
questions.selected: Option 1: Composition is needed because class File depends on Folder.
questions.llm_raw_response:
Option 1: Composition is needed because class File depends on Folder.  
This option aligns with the domain description, which states that every resource (including Files) belongs to a Folder and cannot exist outside of a Folder.

questions.question:
Should the relationship between Folder and Folder be modeled as a composition or as an association?

For example:
1. Use composition, Folder would depend on Folder for its existence.
2. Use association, Folder could exist independently of Folder, and can also be linked to multiple folders.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: Composition is needed because class Folder depends on Folder.
questions.option_2: Option 2: Association is sufficient because class Folder exists independently of Folder.
questions.selected: Option 1: Composition is needed because class Folder depends on Folder.

questions.question:
What is the minimum cardinality of Folder in the relationship with File?

For example:
1. Minimum cardinality of zero, a file can exist without any associated folder.
2. Minimum cardinality of one, a file must have at least one associated folder.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: Zero as minimum cardinality, File can exist without Folder.
questions.option_2: Option 2: One as minimum cardinality,File must have at least one Folder.
questions.selected: Option 2: One as minimum cardinality,File must have at least one Folder.

questions.question:
Should Access be modeled as an abstract class or as a concrete class?
Consider whether Access can be instantiated directly, or only through its subclasses (Edit).

For example:
1. Concrete class  Access ithat can be instantiated. Class accesses can exist independently.
2. Abstract class  Access that cannot be instantiated. Every access must be a specific type edit.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: Concrete class Access that can be instantiated directly.
questions.option_2: Option 2: Abstract class Access, where only the subclasses can be instantiated.
questions.selected: Option 1: Concrete class Access that can be instantiated directly.
